lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Where the devil should this Romeo be?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Came he not home to-night?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Not to his father’s; I spoke with his man.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Ah, that same pale hard-hearted wench, that Rosaline,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Torments him so, that he will sure run mad.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Tybalt, the kinsman of old Capulet,

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Hath sent a letter to his father’s house.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: A challenge, on my life.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Romeo will answer it.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Any man that can write may answer a letter.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Nay, he will answer the letter’s master, how he dares, being dared.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Alas poor Romeo! he is already dead: stabbed with a white wench’s

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: black eye; shot thorough the ear with a love-song; the very pin

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: of his heart cleft with the blind bow-boy’s butt-shaft: and is he

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: a man to encounter Tybalt?

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Why, what is Tybalt?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: More than prince of cats, I can tell you. O, he is

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: the courageous captain of complements. He fights as you sing prick-song, keeps

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: time, distance, and proportion; rests me his minim rest, one, two, and

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: the third in your bosom: the very butcher of a silk button,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: a duellist, a duellist; a gentleman of the very first house, of

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: the first and second cause: ah, the immortal passado! the punto reverso!

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: the hai!

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: The what?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: The pox of such antic, lisping, affecting fantasticoes; these new tuners of

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: accents! “By Jesu, a very good blade! a very tall man! a

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: very good whore!” Why, is not this a lamentable thing, grandsire, that

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: we should be thus afflicted with these strange flies, these fashion-mongers, these

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: perdona-mi’s, who stand so much on the new form, that they cannot

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: sit at ease on the old bench? O, their bones, their bones!

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Here comes Romeo, here comes Romeo.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Without his roe, like a dried herring: O flesh, flesh, how art

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: thou fishified! Now is he for the numbers that Petrarch flowed in:

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Laura to his lady was but a kitchen-wench; marry, she had a

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: better love to be-rhyme her; Dido a dowdy; Cleopatra a gipsy; Helen

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: and Hero hildings and harlots; Thisbe a grey eye or so, but

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: not to the purpose. Signior Romeo, bon jour! there’s a French salutation

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: to your French slop. You gave us the counterfeit fairly last night.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Good morrow to you both. What counterfeit did I give you?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: The slip, sir, the slip; can you not conceive?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Pardon, good Mercutio, my business was great; and in such a case

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: as mine a man may strain courtesy.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: That’s as much as to say, such a case as yours constrains

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: a man to bow in the hams.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Meaning, to court’sy.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Thou hast most kindly hit it.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A most courteous exposition.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Nay, I am the very pink of courtesy.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Pink for flower.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Right.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Why, then is my pump well flowered.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Well said: follow me this jest now till thou hast worn out

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: thy pump, that when the single sole of it is worn, the

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: jest may remain after the wearing sole singular.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O single-soled jest, solely singular for the singleness.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Come between us, good Benvolio; my wits faint.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Switch and spurs, switch and spurs; or I’ll cry a match.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Nay, if thy wits run the wild-goose chase, I have done, for

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: thou hast more of the wild-goose in one of thy wits than,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: I am sure, I have in my whole five: was I with

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: you there for the goose?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thou wast never with me for anything when thou wast not there for the goose.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: I will bite thee by the ear for that jest.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Nay, good goose, bite not.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Thy wit is a very bitter sweeting; it is a most sharp sauce.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And is it not well served in to a sweet goose?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: O here’s a wit of cheveril, that stretches from an inch narrow

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: to an ell broad!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I stretch it out for that word “broad;” which added to the

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: goose, proves thee far and wide a broad goose.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Why, is not this better now than groaning for love? now art

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: thou sociable, now art thou Romeo; now art thou what thou art,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: by art as well as by nature: for this drivelling love is

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: like a great natural, that runs lolling up and down to hide

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: his bauble in a hole.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Stop there, stop there.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Thou desirest me to stop in my tale against the hair.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Thou wouldst else have made thy tale large.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: O, thou art deceived; I would have made it short: for I

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: was come to the whole depth of my tale; and meant, indeed,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: to occupy the argument no longer.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Here’s goodly gear!

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: A sail, a sail!

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: Two, two; a shirt and a smock.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Peter!

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: Anon!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: My fan, Peter.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Good Peter, to hide her face; for her fan’s the fairer face.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: God ye good morrow, gentlemen.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: God ye good den, fair gentlewoman.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Is it good den?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: ’Tis no less, I tell you, for the bawdy hand of the

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: dial is now upon the prick of noon.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Out upon you! what a man are you!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: One, gentlewoman, that God hath made for himself to mar.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: By my troth, it is well said; “for himself to mar,” quoth

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: a’? Gentlemen, can any of you tell me where I may find

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: the young Romeo?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I can tell you; but young Romeo will be older when you

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: have found him than he was when you sought him: I am

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: the youngest of that name, for fault of a worse.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: You say well.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Yea, is the worst well? very well took, i’ faith; wisely, wisely.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: If you be he, sir, I desire some confidence with you.

lines.speaker: BENVOLIO
lines.text: She will indite him to some supper.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: A bawd, a bawd, a bawd! So ho!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What hast thou found?

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: No hare, sir; unless a hare, sir, in a Lenten pie, that

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: is something stale and hoar ere it be spent. An old hare

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: hoar, And an old hare hoar, Is very good meat in Lent:

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: But a hare that is hoar Is too much for a score,

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: When it hoars ere it be spent. Romeo, will you come to

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: your father’s? we’ll to dinner, thither.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I will follow you.

lines.speaker: MERCUTIO
lines.text: Farewell, ancient lady; farewell, “lady, lady, lady.”

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Marry, farewell! I pray you, sir, what saucy merchant was this, that

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: was so full of his ropery?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A gentleman, nurse, that loves to hear himself talk, and will speak

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: more in a minute than he will stand to in a month.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: An a’ speak anything against me, I’ll take him down, an a’

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: were lustier than he is, and twenty such Jacks; and if I

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: cannot, I’ll find those that shall. Scurvy knave! I am none of

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: his flirt-gills; I am none of his skains-mates. And thou must stand

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: by too, and suffer every knave to use me at his pleasure?

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: I saw no man use you at his pleasure; if I had,

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: my weapon should quickly have been out, I warrant you: I dare

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: draw as soon as another man, if I see occasion in a

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: good quarrel, and the law on my side.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Now, afore God, I am so vexed, that every part about me

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: quivers. Scurvy knave! Pray you, sir, a word: and as I told

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: you, my young lady bade me inquire you out; what she bade

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: me say, I will keep to myself: but first let me tell

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: ye, if ye should lead her into a fool’s paradise, as they

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: say, it were a very gross kind of behavior, as they say:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: for the gentlewoman is young; and, therefore, if you should deal double

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: with her, truly it were an ill thing to be offered to

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: any gentlewoman, and very weak dealing.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Nurse, commend me to thy lady and mistress. I protest unto thee—

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Good heart, and, i’ faith, I will tell her as much: Lord,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Lord, she will be a joyful woman.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What wilt thou tell her, nurse? thou dost not mark me.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I will tell her, sir, that you do protest; which, as I

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: take it, is a gentlemanlike offer.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Bid her devise

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Some means to come to shrift this afternoon;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And there she shall at Friar Laurence’ cell

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Be shrived and married. Here is for thy pains.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: No, truly, sir; not a penny.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Go to; I say you shall.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: This afternoon, sir? well, she shall be there.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And stay, good nurse, behind the abbey wall:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Within this hour my man shall be with thee

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And bring thee cords made like a tackled stair;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Which to the high top-gallant of my joy

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Must be my convoy in the secret night.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Farewell; be trusty, and I’ll quit thy pains:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Farewell; commend me to thy mistress.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Now God in heaven bless thee! Hark you, sir.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What say’st thou, my dear nurse?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Is your man secret? Did you ne’er hear say,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Two may keep counsel, putting one away?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I warrant thee, my man’s as true as steel.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Well, sir; my mistress is the sweetest lady—Lord, Lord! when ’twas a

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: little prating thing:—O, there is a nobleman in town, one Paris, that

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: would fain lay knife aboard; but she, good soul, had as lief

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: see a toad, a very toad, as see him. I anger her

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: sometimes and tell her that Paris is the properer man; but, I’ll

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: warrant you, when I say so, she looks as pale as any

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: clout in the versal world. Doth not rosemary and Romeo begin both

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: with a letter?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Ay, nurse; what of that? both with an R.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Ah, mocker! that’s the dog’s name; R is for the—No; I know

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: it begins with some other letter:—and she hath the prettiest sententious of

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: it, of you and rosemary, that it would do you good to

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: hear it.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Commend me to thy lady.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Ay, a thousand times. Peter!

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: Anon!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Peter, take my fan, and go before, and apace.

glosses.definition: Rosaline still torments Romeo (Mercutio's mistaken assumption)
accept and respond to Tybalt's challenge

glosses.phrase: Any man that can write may answer a letter
glosses.definition: Mercutio's pun on 'answer' as reply versus duel
the center-peg of a target; Mercutio's archery metaphor
a blunt practice arrow — for shooting at butts, not in war
Tybalt = Tybert, the cat in the medieval Reynard fables — Mercutio mocks him by name
music sung from written notes — Tybalt fights like a score-reader
he can prick a button off at will — ironically precise
a man of the formal dueling school
highest rank of duelist
a forward thrust in fencing (Italian)
backhanded thrust (Italian)
a plague on; to hell with
foreign fops; affected gallants
old man — Mercutio parodies old-men's complaints
odd, buzzing foreigners

glosses.phrase: stand so much on the new form
glosses.definition: cling so hard to the latest fashion
on the customary old ways
Mercutio's pun — 'bon/bones' playing on French-adopted fashion speech
a fish's eggs — Mercutio puns: Romeo without Ro, the female fish's eggs
14th-century Italian poet who immortalized Laura in sonnets — the model Romeo's affected love imitates
Petrarch's beloved; symbol of unattainable chaste beauty
low servant woman — Mercutio demotes Rosaline
legendary queen of Carthage, lover of Aeneas
dowdy woman; plain wench
queen of Egypt, lover of Antony — another classical beauty topped by Rosaline in Romeo's view
Egyptian (period usage); also vagrant
Helen of Troy
the priestess Leander swam the Hellespont for, at Sestos
low women; whores
Pyramus's lover in Ovid; chosen as a grey-eyed pattern of beauty
prized color in Elizabethan beauty standards
counterfeit coin — Romeo 'slipped' them by vanishing last night
thighs; knees — bawdy wordplay
thin dancing shoe
pursue the witticism to its end
a kind of horseback contest (origin of 'wild goose chase')
fool; silly bird — Mercutio's continuing pun
extensive goose — pun on 'broad' meaning obvious
natural fool; idiot
a jester's ornamented stick (bawdy pun)
mock-nautical announcement of the Nurse's approach (her skirts like sails)
a pair — shirt and smock, man and woman
good afternoon (after noon); contraction of 'good e'en'
the mark on a sundial pointing the hour — bawdy pun

glosses.phrase: A bawd, a bawd, a bawd!
glosses.definition: procurer; pimp — Mercutio mocks the Nurse as a go-between
procurer; go-between for lovers
mouldy; old (pun on 'whore')
midday meal, not evening
a rude fellow; here = scoundrel
loose women; flighty girls
rough companions of the knife-crowd (uncertain)
false hopes; a deceived happiness
dishonest conduct
solemnly declare
confession — Juliet's pretext for meeting Romeo at the Friar's
be confessed and married — the Friar will do both
rope ladder — like a ship's rigging
the highest masthead — Romeo's bedroom window
silent; trustworthy

glosses.phrase: Two may keep counsel, putting one away
glosses.definition: Nurse's joke: two keep a secret if one is dead
chattering; prattling
cloth; rag
the whole universe (Nurse's malapropism)
herb of remembrance, associated with weddings and funerals
R was called the 'dog letter' for its growling sound

annotations.title: Mercutio at full bore — the play's verbal comedy before it ends
annotations.body: 2.4 is Mercutio's great comic aria — the Italian-fencing parody (Tybalt as 'the Prince of Cats,' the 'passado, the punto reverso, the hai!'), the pun-relay with Romeo, the exasperating Nurse-tease. This is the last scene Shakespeare gives him at full linguistic power. From 3.1 forward, the play will have to do without this voice. Notice that Mercutio's style — accelerated puns, Italianism, classical allusion, sexual innuendo — is precisely the mode the tragedy will have to leave behind to become tragic. His killing is also a killing of the play's comic-verbal register.
sources: Jonathan Bate, The Soul of the Age (2008)

trials.prompt: What is Mercutio's running joke about Tybalt in this scene?
options: That Tybalt is 'the Prince of Cats' — Tybert, the cat of medieval Reynard fables — and fights by the Italian fencing-manual
That Tybalt is in love with Rosaline

trials.prompt: Why is Mercutio's comic voice structurally important to Romeo and Juliet?
options: Mercutio's language represents the comic-verbal mode the tragedy will have to abandon once he dies in 3.1
